summary:
The Communist Manifesto, published in February 1848 by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels on behalf of the Communist League, is one of the most influential political pamphlets ever written. Its analysis of history as driven by class struggle and its call for the working class to seize political power launched a tradition of political thought that shaped revolutionary movements, governments, and global politics throughout the twentieth century.

Marx and Engels wrote the Manifesto in the context of the industrial capitalism just transforming European society and the [[event:2532|revolutionary atmosphere of 1848]]. Their central argument was that all recorded history was the history of class struggle — between masters and slaves, lords and serfs, bourgeoisie and proletariat. They argued that industrial capitalism had simplified this struggle to its essential form: the bourgeoisie (owners of the means of production) versus the proletariat (workers who sold their labor). Capitalism, they argued, was inherently self-destructive: it created the very class — the industrial working class — that would overthrow it.

The Manifesto's immediate political program called for workers of all countries to unite, the overthrow of capitalist class rule, and the establishment of common ownership of the means of production. Its famous concluding lines — 'Workers of the world, unite!' — became the rallying cry of the international labor movement. The short-term impact was modest; the revolutions of 1848 largely failed and Marx spent decades as an exile in London writing his major theoretical work Das Kapital.

The Manifesto's long-term impact was enormous. The Russian Revolution of 1917, the Chinese Revolution of 1949, and revolutionary movements across Latin America, Africa, and Asia claimed its ideas as their inspiration. By the mid-twentieth century, roughly one-third of humanity lived under governments claiming to act in its name. The theories Marx and Engels developed — historical materialism, surplus value, the class analysis of politics — became central frameworks in academic social science even among those who rejected Marxism politically. The Manifesto remains one of the most widely read and debated political texts ever written.
